content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcar- PRACrrcBs CoMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 c 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-s660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
August 20,2002
George Fuller
2694East Garvey Avenue, South
Suite 343
West Covina, CA 91791-2113
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-02-189
Dear Mr. Fuller:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest and disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note
that the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. (Regulations
18329(bX8)(A) and (c)(a)(A).) Therefore, any conclusions contained herein apply only to
prospective actions. We are advising only on your future campaign reporting requirements
and possible conflicts of interest.
QUESTTONS
1. Do you report the lodging and subsistence payments from the Teachers Association of
West Covina ("TAWC") as a gift? Or could the payments be considered necessary lodging and
subsistence to a panel participant?
2. If the payment is a gift, are you required to recuse yourself from all decisions involving
the donor' TAwc? 
.'NCLUSI,NS
1. Since the payment for lodging and subsistence is past conduct, we cannot give advice
concerning whether it was a gift.. We do include general advice to guide you to determine for
yourself whether the payment constitutes a gift.
2. Assuming that the payment was a gift, then you are required to recuse yourself from
decisions directly involving the donor, TAWC.
' Gove*ment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-
18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
Ps4
Re


File No. I-02-189
Page No. 2
FACTS
You are an elected member of the West Covina Unified School District ("WCUSD')
Board of Trustees. As you are employed as a teacher at another school district, and you are also
a member of the California Teachers Association ("CTA") and the National Education
Association ('NEA"). In the spring of 2002, NEA invited you to attend a NEA-sponsored
education conference in Colorado Springs, Colorado on March 16- 1 8, 2002 - the "Challenge of
Change 2002: Leadership and the Human Side of School Change" conference. This conference
is an annual event, open to teachers, administrators, parents, sfudents, city officials and school
board members.
This year, NEA offered to pay for airfare, but the board decided not to cover the lodging
and subsistence expenses. The Teachers Association of West Covina ("TAWC"), a local chapter
of CTA, offered to pay your lodging and subsistence expenses for the conference, which
amounted to approximately $650. The TAWC desired your attendance at the conference in order
that you could participate in the anticipated breakout panel sessions.
TAWC is a non-profrt organization with annual receipts of approximately $48,000.
Individual teacher membership dues are approximately $120 per year. TAWC is the exclusive
bargaining agent for teachers within the WCUSD. TAWC comes before the WCUSD Board of
Trustees when negotiating and adopting collective bargaining agreements, ratiffing settlements
agreement between the school district and TAWC, dispositions of grievances under the
collective bargaining agreement, and determinations of individual teacher dismissal or discipline
where TAWC is representing a teacher. TAWC is impacted by decisions of the WCUSD Board
of Trustees concerning teacher hiring, reductions in work force, and teacher dismissals or
discipline (even though TAWC is not representing the teacher).
On August 16,2002, you provided additional facts regarding the types of decisions that
you would be making on the WCUSD Board of Trustees. These would include hiring freezes
and personnel matters involving both represented and uffepresented employees.
ANALYSIS
Gifts
Because the subject matter of your questions relate to past conduct, we can only address
the questions regarding prospective reporting and possible conflicts of interest. We have
included copies of sections of the Act and regulations that may assist you with your questions
pertaining to the receipt of gifts and gift limits. Below is a general analysis of the gift laws.
The Act defines the term "gift" as any payment for personal purposes to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received." (Section 82028.) The term "payment" is
defined in section 82044 to include "a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift
or other rendering of money, property, services or anything else of value, whether tangible or
intangible."


File No. I-02-189
Page No. 3
Regulation 18950.3 provides an exception related to travel, which is not applicable to
your facts.
Disclosure
The Act requires that every public official disclose all his or her economic interests that
could foreseeably be affected by the exercise of the official's duties. (Sections 81002(c) and
87200.) It is your responsibility to report the source of any gift $50 or more and to include the
name, address, and general description of the business activity of each source. (Section 57207.)
In addition, pursuant to section 81004 all reports and statements required to be filed by
the Act must be signed under penalty of perjury and verified by the filer. The verification must
state that the filer has used all reasonable diligence in its preparation and that to the best of his or
her knowledge it is true and complete. (Solorio Advice Letter, No. 4-97-092-) You are required
to report all gifts of $50 or more on your Statement of Economic Interests (Form 700).
Section 82028(b)(2) states that the term "gift" does not include:
"Gifts which are not used and which, within 30 days after
receipt, are either returned to the donor or delivered to a nonprofit
entity exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal
Revenue Code without being claimed as a charitable conhibution
for tax purposes."
Pursuant to regulation 18941, "a gift is 'received' or'accepted' when the recipient knows
that he or she has either acttal possession of the gift or takes any action exercising direction or
control over the gift." Regulation 18943 sets out how to apply section 82028(b)(2) and provides
that:
"(a) General Rule for Retum, Donation, or Reimbursement of a
Gift. A gift is neither accepted nor received if it is returned
donated, or reimbursed in any manner set forth below:
(1) 
The gift is returned to the donor, or to the donor's
agent or intermediary, unused, within 30 days of receipt or
acceptance. In such event, neither the recipient nor the donor shall
be required to disclose the receipt or making of a gift or activity
expense; or
fl... fl
(4) 
The recipient, within 30 days of receipt or
acceptance, reimburses the donor, or the donor's agent or
intermediary, for all or a portion of the gift. In such event, the
value of the gift is reduced by the amount of the reimbursement,


File No. l-02-189
Page No. 4
and the amount of any gift or activity expense which must be
disclosed is reduced by the amount of the reimbursement."
Gift Limits
You are also subject to a gift limit of $320 from a single source in a calendar year.
(Section 89503.) Section 89506 and regulation I 8950.1 provide for certain exceptions to the gift
limit and have been enclosed for your edification. 
'
Conflicts of Interest
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their financial interests. (Section
81001(b).) Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from "making,"
"participating in making," or otherwise using his or her official position to "influence" a
govemmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Your question concems whether you must recuse yourself from decisions involving
TAWC. To determine if you have a disqualifying conflict of interest that would limit your
involvement in decisions of the school board of trustees, we apply the s-tandard eight-step
analysis the Commission has adopted. (Regulation 18700 OXl) - (8).)' For purposes oithis
letter we will assume that you received a gift from TAWC.
One
"influencing" a governmental decision?
Under the Act, a "public official" is defined, inpart, as a "member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." (Section 82048.) As a member of West
Covina Unified School District Board of Trustees, you are a public official and subject to the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. Your request for advice presupposes that you will at
least participate in making governmental decisions regarding TAWC. Having disposed of these
preliminaries, we advance to the third step of the analysis, identif,uing your economic interests.
Step Three: Do you have an economic interest?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from
economic interests. There are six kinds of such economic interests, however, you have only
asked about gifts. A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(e);
regulation I8703.4). Assuming that the $650 was a gift, then you have a potentially
disquali$zing economic interest. 
3
2 These steps are addressed in the FPPC document entitled, "Can I Vote? Conflicts of Interest Overyiew,,
which is enclosed for your information.
3 To determine if payments from TAWC must be cumulated with any gifts from CTA, apply regulation
18945.1 . For purposes of this analysis, we are assuming regulation 18945 ,l does not apply to CTA. (Regulation
18945.1, enclosed.)


File No. I-02-189
Page No. 5
Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
The next step is to determine whether the economic interests will be involved directly or
indirectly in the decision. Determining whether the public official's economic interest is directly
or indirectly involved in the governmental decision is important because it helps determine
which materiality test to use in deciding whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have amateial financial effect on the economic interest. (Regulation
18700(bx4).)
Regulation I 87 04.1 (a) provides
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an
official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
conceming the decision before the official or the official,s agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person."
Therefore, if the TAWC comprises an economic interest to you, then that economic
interest will be directly involved in the decision when it is an applicant or a named parly, such as
when TAWC negotiates collective bargaining agreements or is a party to a settlement agreement
with the school district. (Regulation 18705.1(b).) A person who is a source of a gift is deemed
to be indirectly involved if they do not fall into one of the preceding categories. (Regulation
18704.1(a).) This would include, for example, TAWC representing a teacher in dismissals or
disciplinary proceedings and decisions conceming the expansion and reduction of the teaching
staff (where TAWC is not aparty). (Ball Advice Letter, No. I-99-041 and Petzold Advice Letter,
No. 4-96-191.) Generally, in these types of situations, you would apply regulation 18705.4(b).
Steps Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on )rour economic interest be
materialitv and reasonably foreseeable?
Once you identiS your relevant economic interests, you must evaluate whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial effect on any of those
economic interests by ascertaining the applicable materiality standard (regulations 18700(b)(5),
18705 - 18705.5) and then determining whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the standard will
be met. (Regulations 18700(bX6), 1 8706.) An effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required, but the effect must be
more than a mere possibility. (Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Comm.


File No. I-02-189
Page No. 6
(1987) 196 cal.App.3d 983, 9891' witt v. Monow (1977) 70 cal.App.3d 817,822; Harper
Advice Letter, No. 4-96-298.)
"Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect upon a person which is a source of gift to a
public official, and which person is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is
deemed mateial." (Regulation 18705.4(a).) This is also known as the "one-penny" rule. For an
indirectly involved non-profit entity, such as TAWC, which is a source of a gift, the applicable
materiality standard is found at regulation 18705.4(b)(2):
"Sources of gifts which are indirectly involved nonprofit entities or
govemment agencies. If the source of a gift is a nonprofit entity or
a govemment agency, ?pply the materiality standards stated
in. . .section 1 8705.3(bX2)."
The thresholds in the regulation vary depending upon the gross annual receipts of the
nonprofit organization. Regulation 1 8705. 3 (bX2), for exampl", .tut"r t
"...The effect of a decision is material as to a nonprofit entity
which is a source of income to the official if any of the following
applies:
"11...fl
"(F) For an entity whose gross annual receipts are $ 100,000 or
less, the effect of the decision will be any of the following:
"(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the
entity's gross annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of
$10,000 or more.
"(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid
additional expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses
for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 ormore.
"(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of $10,000
or more. "
We cannot determine which mateiality standard in the regulation is relevant to your facts
because we do not know what decision will be in question. You will have to apply the
appropriate standard when a situation arises, or you can write in for advice on a specific decision.
Steps seven and Eieht: Public generally and legally required exceptions.
We have not gone on to discuss the latter two steps in the standard conflict-of-interest
analysis. Step seven is an exception that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and material
financial effect on the official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the


File No. I-02-189
page No. 7
public generally, and step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally required
to participate in the decision. You have not provided facts to indicate if these rules are
applicable to your situation, but we have enclosed copies of these regulations for your
information.
Segmentation
In specific situations, a public official disqualified from one decision may
participate in other related decisions provided that the official's participation does not
affect the decision in which he or she has a conflict of interest. (In re Owen (1976) 2
FPPC Ops.77.) Certain decisions are too interrelated to be considered separately and a
public official's conflict on one decision will cause disqualification for the other.
"Decisions are inextricably interrelated where, among other
things, one decision is a necessary condition precedent or condition
subsequent for another. Thus, a public official would have to
disqualify himself or herself if the result of one decision would
effectively determine or nullify the result of another." (Woodruff
Advice Letter, No. A-01-157.)
The public body must procedurally segregate a decision that can be logically
divided prior to allowing the public official with a related conflict to participate in the
decision-making process. This is achieved through three steps:
"(1) the decisions in which the public official has a disqualifuing
financial interest should be segregated from the other decisions on
the public body's agenda;
(2) the decisions from which the public official is disqualified
should be considered first, and a final decision should be reached
by the public body without the disqualified official's participation
in any way; and
(3) once a decision has been reached on the issues in which the
official is disqualified, the disqualified official may participate in
the deliberations regarding the other related issues so long as his or
her participation does not result in a reopening of the previous
issues or in any other way affect the decisions concerning the
previous issues in which the public official was disqualified from
participation." (Ibid.)
Segmentation may be appropriate in your case for personnel matters that involve both
represented and unrepresented employees. You would have to apply the three steps provided
above to the decision before your possible participation.


File No. I-02-189
Page No. 8
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
Galena West
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures
Gwie

decision-making process. This is achieved through three steps:
"(1) the decisions in which the public official has a disqualifuing
financial interest should be segregated from the other decisions on
the public body's agenda;
(2) the decisions from which the public official is disqualified
should be considered first, and a final decision should be reached
by the public body without the disqualified official's participation
in any way; and
(3) once a decision has been reached on the issues in which the
official is disqualified, the disqualified official may participate in
the deliberations regarding the other related issues so long as his or
her participation does not result in a reopening of the previous
issues or in any other way affect the decisions concerning the
previous issues in which the public official was disqualified from
participation." (Ibid.)
Segmentation may be appropriate in your case for personnel matters that involve both
represented and unrepresented employees. You would have to apply the three steps provided
above to the decision before your possible participation.

QUESTION: 1. Do you report the lodging and subsistence payments from the Teachers Association of
West Covina ("TAWC") as a gift? Or could the payments be considered necessary lodging and
subsistence to a panel participant?
2. If the payment is a gift, are you required to recuse yourself from all decisions involving
the donor' TAwc? 
.'NCLUSI,NS
1. Since the payment for lodging and subsistence is past conduct, we cannot give advice
concerning whether it was a gift.. We do include general advice to guide you to determine for
yourself whether the payment constitutes a gift.
2. Assuming that the payment was a gift, then you are required to recuse yourself from
decisions directly involving the donor, TAWC.

Box 807 c 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807 (916) 322-s660 o Fax (916) 322-0886 August 20,2002 George Fuller 2694East Garvey Avenue, South Suite 343 West Covina, CA 91791-2113 Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-02-189 Dear Mr. Fuller: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of- interest and disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. (Regulations 18329(bX8)(A) and (c)(a)(A).) Therefore, any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions. We are advising only on your future campaign reporting requirements and possible conflicts of interest. QUESTTONS 1. Do you report the lodging and subsistence payments from the Teachers Association of West Covina ("TAWC") as a gift? Or could the payments be considered necessary lodging and subsistence to a panel participant? 2. If the payment is a gift, are you required to recuse yourself from all decisions involving the donor' TAwc? .'NCLUSI,NS 1. Since the payment for lodging and subsistence is past conduct, we cannot give advice concerning whether it was a gift.. We do include general advice to guide you to determine for yourself whether the payment constitutes a gift. 2. Assuming that the payment was a gift, then you are required to recuse yourself from decisions directly involving the donor, TAWC. ' Gove*ment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109- 18997, of the California Code of Regulations. Ps4 Re File No. I-02-189 Page No. 2 FACTS You are an elected member of the West Covina Unified School District ("WCUSD') Board of Trustees. As you are employed as a teacher at another school district, and you are also a member of the California Teachers Association ("CTA") and the National Education Association ('NEA"). In the spring of 2002, NEA invited you to attend a NEA-sponsored education conference in Colorado Springs, Colorado on March 16- 1 8, 2002 - the "Challenge of Change 2002: Leadership and the Human Side of School Change" conference. This conference is an annual event, open to teachers, administrators, parents, sfudents, city officials and school board members. This year, NEA offered to pay for airfare, but the board decided not to cover the lodging and subsistence expenses. The Teachers Association of West Covina ("TAWC"), a local chapter of CTA, offered to pay your lodging and subsistence expenses for the conference, which amounted to approximately $650. The TAWC desired your attendance at the conference in order that you could participate in the anticipated breakout panel sessions. TAWC is a non-profrt organization with annual receipts of approximately $48,000. Individual teacher membership dues are approximately $120 per year. TAWC is the exclusive bargaining agent for teachers within the WCUSD. TAWC comes before the WCUSD Board of Trustees when negotiating and adopting collective bargaining agreements, ratiffing settlements agreement between the school district and TAWC, dispositions of grievances under the collective bargaining agreement, and determinations of individual teacher dismissal
